coredata.dc:title: Iron doped carbon dots based nanohybrids as a tetramodal imaging agent for gene delivery promotion and photothermal-chemodynamic cancer synergistic theranostics

coredata.dc:description: Nonviral gene delivery vectors for cancer therapy still confronts great challenges as their low transfection efficiency, vague tracing and poor therapeutic effects. Recently, organic–inorganic nanohybrids show complementary and synergistic effects in nano-scaled delivery system, inspiring their applications in gene delivery exploration. A liposomal carbon dots nanohybrids system (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) have been fabricated, which was composed of iron-doped carbon dots (Fe@CDs) derived from iron (II) phthalocyanine and amphiphilic lipopeptide assemblies (DSPE-mPEG2000/RLS). With its excellent photothermal conversion capability (η = 63.4%), gene transfection efficiency was increased by 3.5-fold in 4T1 tumor cell and 2-fold in animal model. Introduction of Fe@CDs in the nanohybrids could also serve as a tetramodal contrast agent for fluorescence/photoacoustic/photothermal/magnetic resonance imaging, showing up to 3 orders of magnitude enhancement in photoacoustic signal, as well as 16 °C temperature increasement after 5 min irradiation inside tumor tissue. Furthermore, both in vitro and in vivo studies confirmed the bioactivity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as nanozyme for photothermal and chemodynamic synergistic therapy, leading to a noticeable inhibited tumor growth with 80% survival for at least 50 days in a breast tumor model. These revealed the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs is a promising candidate platform for improving gene therapy by transfection enhancement, real-time tracing and tumor synergistic destruction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization and optical properties of Fe@CDs and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. A) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Fe@CDs (inset: the corresponding size distribution calculated by Nano Measurer software). B) The structure of Fe@CDs revealed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image. C) High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of Fe 2p for the Fe@CDs. D) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the FePc and Fe@CDs. E) Fluorescence emission spectra of Fe@CDs and FePc in DMF, and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in H2O. F) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe@CDs and FePc in DMF, and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in H2O. G) TEM image of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. H) The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in aqueous solution (inset: ζ potentials of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in aqueous solution).

Characterization of photothermal and peroxidase (POD)-like properties of the as-prepared PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. A) Temperature variation profile of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs under 660 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min). B) Photothermal stability of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (300 µg/mL) with five cycled irradiations (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min) separated by a 10 min cooling phase. C) Calculated orbital distributions and energy levels for the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lower their lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) plots of FePc and Pc at their optimized ground state geometries. D) UV–vis absorption spectra of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), TMB/H2O2, and TMB/PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/H2O2 for 30 min (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, 4 μg/mL; TMB, 80 μg/mL; H2O2, 40 mM). E) Methylene blue (MB) degradation by generated •OH under different experimental conditions for 30 min (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, 40 μg/mL; MB, 10 μg/mL; H2O2, 40 mM). F) Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of different reaction condition with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trap for 3 h (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, 40 μg/mL; 10 μL of DMPO; H2O2, 400 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Photothermally enhanced EGFP transfection of various complexes on the 4T1 cell lines. Fluorescence microscopy images of transfected 4T1 cells at various N/P ratios without (A) or with (C) 660 nm laser. Semi-quantitative analysis of the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) from the microscopy images of transfected cells without (B) or with (D) 660 nm laser using Image J software. Scale bars are 100 μm. n = 5; **P < 0.01.

In vitro tetramodal imaging with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. A) Confocal fluorescence (FL) images of 4T1 cells incubated with the PEG-RLS/CDs (20 μg/mL) and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively. Scale bars: 5 µm. B) Longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T1) of the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. C) T1-weighted magnetic resonance (MR) images with different concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in agarose phantoms. D) Photoacoustic (PA) images of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs aqueous solution with different concentrations in agarose tubes. E) PA intensity of carbon dots assemblies at indicated concentration. F) The in vitro photothermal (PT) images of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs solutions under irradiation of 660 nm laser for 10 min at indicated concentrations. G) Temperature variation of carbon dots assemblies with different concentration irradiated by 660 nm laser at power density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min.

In vitro photothermal-enhanced chemical dynamic therapy (CDT) of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. A) Relative cell viability of different treatments for with varying concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. The different treatments group as follows: Relative cell viability of L929 a) and 4T1 cells b) with different concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs treatment for 24 h in dark conditions. c) Relative viability of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs for 4 h and then irradiated with 660 nm laser for 10 min following by another 24 h continuous cultivation. d) Viability of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs and H2O2 (50 μM) for 4 h following by another 24 h continuous cultivation. e) Viability of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs and H2O2 (50 μM) and then irradiated with 660 nm laser for 10 min following by another 24 h continuous cultivation. B) Viability of 4T1 cell cultured with varying H2O2 concentrations and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h following by another 24 h continuous cultivation. C) Confocal images of 4T1 cells after various treatments with 50 μM H2O2, 100 μg/mL PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, and H2O2 + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs stained with 2, 7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), the scale bars are 50 μm. D) Confocal images of fluorescein diacetate (FDA)/propidium iodide (PI) costained 4T1 cells after incubation with different formulations, scale bars are 100 µm.

In vivo tetramodal imaging with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (25 mg/kg). A) Real-time in vivo fluorescence (FL) images after i.v. injection of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in mice at different time points. B) Statistical analysis of the FL intensities in tumors site after i.v. injection of PEG- RLS/Fe@CDs. C) The time-dependent in vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging of tumor sites after intravenous administration of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. D) The time-dependent PA average signal intensities of tumor sites. E) In vivo photothermal (PT) imaging of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs under 660 nm light irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) at different times after 28 h post-injection. F) The temperature variation profile of tumor sites under 660 nm light irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) at different times after 28 h post-injection. G) In vivo T1-weighted magnetic resonance (MR) imaging for 0, 6, 28 and 32 h after intravenous injection of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. The T1 weighted MR images were acquired using the following parameters: TR = 530 ms, TE = 30 ms, field-of-view (FOV) = 35 × 35 mm2, and thickness = 1 mm. The white circles showed the position of the tumor. H) Relative T1-weighted MR signals of tumors based on the MR images.

In vivo photo-enhanced therapeutic effect of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. After 28 h post-injection, the tumor tissues were irradiated by 660 nm laser (0.5 w/cm2) for 5 min in different groups. A) Relative tumor growth curves in different groups during 16 days of treatments. B) Photographs of tumors removed from the sacrificed mice after the last treatment. C) Survival curves of 4T1-tumor-bearing mice after different treatments. D) Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain assay of tumor tissues in different groups after the last treatment. Scale bars are 250 μm. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs were intravenously injected to the mice at 25 mg/kg. E) The fluorescence images of 4T1 frozen tumor sections after injection of pEGFP complexes for 48 h. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

Schematic illustration of the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as cancer nanotheranostic platform for photothermal responsive gene delivery, and tetramodal imaging (fluorescence &magnetic resonance, photothermal & photoacoustic imaging)-guided photothermal-chemodynamic synergistic therapy in solid tumors.

head.ce:title: Iron doped carbon dots based nanohybrids as a tetramodal imaging agent for gene delivery promotion and photothermal-chemodynamic cancer synergistic theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nonviral gene delivery vectors for cancer therapy still confronts great challenges as their low transfection efficiency, vague tracing and poor therapeutic effects. Recently, organic–inorganic nanohybrids show complementary and synergistic effects in nano-scaled delivery system, inspiring their applications in gene delivery exploration. A liposomal carbon dots nanohybrids system (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) have been fabricated, which was composed of iron-doped carbon dots (Fe@CDs) derived from iron (II) phthalocyanine and amphiphilic lipopeptide assemblies (DSPE-mPEG2000/RLS). With its excellent photothermal conversion capability (η = 63.4%), gene transfection efficiency was increased by 3.5-fold in 4T1 tumor cell and 2-fold in animal model. Introduction of Fe@CDs in the nanohybrids could also serve as a tetramodal contrast agent for fluorescence/photoacoustic/photothermal/magnetic resonance imaging, showing up to 3 orders of magnitude enhancement in photoacoustic signal, as well as 16 °C temperature increasement after 5 min irradiation inside tumor tissue. Furthermore, both in vitro and in vivo studies confirmed the bioactivity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as nanozyme for photothermal and chemodynamic synergistic therapy, leading to a noticeable inhibited tumor growth with 80% survival for at least 50 days in a breast tumor model. These revealed the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs is a promising candidate platform for improving gene therapy by transfection enhancement, real-time tracing and tumor synergistic destruction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Non-viral vectors-based gene therapy has shown great potential on the malignant cancer destruction. However, the translation of these achievements to the clinical applications is substantially dependent on the development of efficient and safe delivery systems [1]. Organic-inorganic nanohybrids are one of the most attractive fields in materials science these days, due to their integration of the benefits from both organic and inorganic components. It could show stimuli-responsiveness, targeting, and controlled release manner from organic molecules, and combine with desirable optical, magnetic, and electrical properties from inorganic ones. Thus, the nanohybrids based theranostic gene delivery system has gain much attentions [2]. Iron oxide nanoparticles is the earliest inorganic nanoparticles used in this field, profited by their efficacy on enhanced MRI imaging, magnetic targeting and synergistic magnetic hyperthermia property [3–6]. Other inorganic components, such as graphene oxide, gold nanorod and MoS2, are also successfully developed to support gene delivery with improved therapy and diagnosis effect [2]. Meanwhile, both our and others’ researches have solidified the fact that these inorganic nanoparticles not only assist the gene complexes to cross multiple barriers, but also improved therapy performance by synergistic bioactivities (such as tumor associated macrophage polarization regulation, etc.) [7].

Inorganic nanoparticles with photothermal property are consider to be of great value, as it might promote cellular uptake, endosomal escape and gene release [8,9]. Meanwhile, photothermal therapy and imaging could provide more intensive tumor killing and metabolism information [10–14], facilitating the design of more efficient gene delivery carrier. However, single photothermal imaging still struggles to fulfil the spatial resolution requirement, which usually improved by other imaging agents addition and leads to a complicated design and synthesis with higher costs.

Thus, a special inorganic material, carbon dots (CDs) originated from the organic molecules attracted our attention, which are reported to be much suitable for a facile “all-in-one” theranostic agent in multi-modal imaging and diversified combination therapy [14–18]. With specifically selected precursors, CDs can integrate advantages of varied imaging modalities into a single platform. The consequent multi-modal imaging system could bring more precise and accurate information about bioactive molecule distribution and metabolism. And CDs based nanoprobes show great potential on tumor killing with assistance of photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy (PTT), or nanozyme for chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [19–21].

Our previous study showed CDs alone could only achieve comparably lower gene transfection efficiency than the commercially available reagent [22]. While on the other hand, with inspiration from virus, many relatively successful organic molecules have been designed, exhibiting preeminent performance during gene delivery [23–26]. Based on the experiences from both organic and inorganic carriers, it is reasonable to engage both advantages into one system to promote the gene therapy.

Considering the importance of CDs precursor, the iron (II) phthalocyanine (FePc), an intrinsic heteroatom containing compound, was herein selected with expectation of a facile “all-in-one” iron-doped carbon dots (Fe@CDs). Thereafter, the prepared Fe@CDs were functionalized with DSPE-mPEG2000 and RLS (a dendritic arginine and disulfide bond-containing cationic lipopeptide)[7,23–26] by self-assembly. The optimal mass and N/P ratio for gene transfection, as well as irradiation condition were screened on breast cancer cells. The absorption and emission spectra, photothermal conversion efficiency, photostability, multi-model (FL/PA/PT/MR) imaging potential, PTT capacity and peroxidase (POD)-like nanozyme catalytic ability of the complexes were all successively evaluated in vitro. Tumor synergistic inhibition effect was pre-detected in breast cancer 4T1 cells in various conditions, and a further xenograft breast tumor model was conducted to evaluate the gene delivery as well as tumor imaging and therapy efficiency of complexes in vivo (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Reagents and materials
ce:section.ce:para: 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), methylene blue (MB), phthalocyanine (Pc), PEI 25 k (branched, average Mw = 25 kDa) and iron (II) phthalocyanine (FePc) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were bought from Adamas (China). 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N- [methoxy (polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-mPEG2000) was bought from AVT Pharmaceutical Tech Co, Ltd (China). Cell culture plates were purchased from Nest Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). The Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), Lipofectamine 2000 (Lipo 2000), RIMP-1640 medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Thermo (USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were purchased from Dojindo (Japan). Enhanced green fluorescent protein encoding plasmid (pEGFP) was obtained using the EndoFree plasmid kit from Qiagen (Germany). Fluorescein diacetate (FDA), propidium iodide (PI), 2, 7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and O.T.C embedding reagent were purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., (China). Deionized water was employed in all tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of iron-doped carbon dots (Fe@CDs) and iron-undoped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Iron (II) phthalocyanine (FePc) (25 mg, 0.044 mmol) was ultrasonically dispersed in 40 mL anhydrous DMF for 30 min. The solution was then added to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (100 mL) for 24 h heating at 180 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained dark green solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and then dialyzed (MWCO 1000 Da) for 3 d. The final product (Fe@CDs) was obtained by lyophilization. As control, undoped carbon dots (CDs) formed by phthalocyanine without Fe was prepared through the same procedures.

Chemical composition and surface states of the products were performed with X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, XSAM 800 spectrometer, Kratos Co., Ltd., UK) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet 380 spectrometer, Thermo, USA). Morphologies of both Fe@CDs and CDs dispersed in DMF solution were photographed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (TecnaiG2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA). UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were obtained from Hitachi U-2900 (Hitachi, Japan) and F-7000 spectrophotometers (Hitachi, Japan), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Fe@CDs nanohybrids (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) and CDs nanohybrids (PEG-RLS/CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, a dendritic arginine and disulfide bond-containing cationic lipopeptide (RLS) was synthesized as previously published [7,23–26], performing efficient gene condensation, cytomembrane penetration and microenvironment responsive release at target sites. To obtain complex, the DSPE-mPEG2000 and RLS assemblies at different weight ratios were evaluated based on gene transfection efficiency (DSPE-mPEG2000 to RLS, P/R = 0.25, 0.5, 1, N/P = 20). After the optimal weight ratio of 0.5 was screened, different volume (5, 10, 30 μL) of Fe@CDs (50 mg/mL) were mixed homogeneously with 10 μL cationic lipopeptide (RLS) (50 mg/mL) and 5 μL DSPE-mPEG2000 (50 mg/mL). Then, the mixture was added drop-by-drop into deionized water to form DSPE-mPEG2000/RLS/Fe@CDs (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) nanohybrids. CDs encapsulated in DSPE-mPEG2000 and RLS assemblies (PEG-RLS/CDs) were prepared as control. Size distribution and zeta potential of samples were determined by Zetasizer Nano ZS system (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). The morphologies of samples were observed by TEM.

PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/DNA complexes at different N/P ratios ranging from 0.5 to 10 (N/P ratio of material relative to DNA) were prepared by adding an appropriate volume of material to gene (200 ng). All samples were electrophoresed on the 1% (w/v) agarose gel containing GelRed at 120 V for 40 min. In addition, DNA release profiles from PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/DNA complexes in reductive environments were further investigated by gel electrophoresis assay after incubation with 10 mM dithiothreitol (DTT) at 37 °C for 2 h. The nucleic acid was visualized using Molecular Imager ChemiDoc XRS + (Bio-Rad, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal property characterizations of carbon dots and carbon dots assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature variation profiles of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs and PEG-RLS/CDs aqueous dispersions, as well as CDs or Fe@CDs in organic solvent (DMF) were investigated at indicated concentrations with a 660 nm laser irradiation for 10 min at 0.5 W/cm2 (MW-GX-660/1 ~ 1300 mW, Changchun Laser Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd., China). Thermal imaging camera (FLIR E60, UK) was applied to record the real-time temperature variations every 30 s. Deionized water was served as control group. Meanwhile, the photothermal stability of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (300 µg/mL, 1 mL) assemblies were determined by five cycled irradiations. Each cycle consisted of 10 min irradiation followed by a 10 min cooling phase.

Photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated according to the methods reported previously [27]. Briefly, the temperature of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs dispersion (300 μg/mL, 1 mL) in a 1.5 mL eppendorf tube was recorded every 30 s by thermal imaging camera under continuous 660 nm laser irradiation at 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. After removing the laser, temperature was continuously measured every 30 s until it reduced to the environmental temperature (about 10 min). The η value of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was calculated according to the following equations, where h is the heat transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the container, T Max is the maximum temperature, T Surr is the surrounding temperature, m is the mass of the system, CH₂O is the heat capacity of solvent, τs is a system time constant, QDis expresses the heat dissipation due to the solvent water light-absorption, I is the laser power, A 660 is the absorbance of the irradiation wavelength at 660 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density functional theory calculations
ce:section.ce:para: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were computed at B3LYP/6-311G(d) and PBE0/6-311G(d) level, respectively [28]. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) distributions were calculated with their optimized ground states. All the calculations are executed by using the Gaussian 09 program package [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal-responsive gene transfection assay
ce:section.ce:para: Transfection assays were performed with pEGFP encoding enhanced green fluorescent protein (EGFP) as reporter genes. Briefly, cells were seeded into the 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells per well). The medium was then replaced by 100 μL fresh medium with 10% serum and various gene complexes at different N/P ratios of 10–40 (containing 200 ng of EGFP plasmid per well) 24 h later. After 4 h incubation at 37 °C, the culture medium was changed with fresh one. For photothermal-assisted transfection, cells were immediately irradiated by the 660 nm laser at power density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. Commercial transfection reagent lipofectamine 2000 (Lipo 2000) and PEI 25 k with or without laser irradiation were served as the control. Cells were continuously cultured for another 20 h and the EGFP-expressing cells were visualized using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Germany) and semi-quantitatively analyzed by calculating the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) using the Image J software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase (POD)-like properties characterizations of carbon dots assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: POD-like properties of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs were first evaluated by the absorbance intensity change of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) after reaction with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in the presence of H2O2 by UV–vis spectra. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs or PEG-RLS/CDs (2 μL, 1.0 mg/mL) was added into 0.5 mL of sodium acetate (NaAc, pH = 4.5) buffer containing 2 μL of H2O2 (30%) and 2 μL of TMB (20 mg/mL). After 30 min reaction at room temperature protecting from light, the absorbance at λ652 nm of the mixture solution was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-2900).

Classical colorimetric method was carried out for the quantitative evaluation of hydroxyl radical (•OH) generation, by calculating the decreased absorbance of methylene blue (MB) at λ664 nm [29]. Briefly, the absorbance value at λ = 664 nm of 475 μL NaAc buffer (pH = 4.5) containing MB (10 μg/mL), with or without 2 μL 30% H2O2 were monitored before and after PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs or PEG-RLS/CDs (20 μL, 1 mg/mL) addition. Meanwhile the effect of temperature on •OH generation was also assessed to simulate the potential photothermal effect with the range of 25–45 °C.

Electron spin resonance (ESR, Bruker, Germany) spectroscopy was employed to confirm the reactive oxygen species (ROS) hydroxyl radical production with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. 10 μL of DMPO was added into the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs assemblies dispersed in 500 μL NaAc buffer (pH = 4.5) with or without 400 μM of H2O2. After 3 h reaction, 2 μL portion of the mixture was detected immediately by ESR spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of cytotoxicity, photothermal therapy (PTT), nanozyme for chemodynamic therapy (CDT), and synergistic PTT/CDT therapy of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs against 4T1 and L929 cells in vitro was detected by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). Briefly, cells were seeded into 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells per well) and treated with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs at indicated concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL). After 24 h incubation, the culture medium was removed, and 10% CCK-8 solution in FBS-free medium was added for 2–4 h reaction in the incubator. The UV–vis absorption was detected at 450 nm.

In order to evaluate the PTT efficacy, the seeded 4T1 cells were firstly incubated with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs for 4 h at indicated concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100 and 200 μg/mL), and then illuminated by 660 nm laser for 10 min at intensity of 0.5 W/cm2. After another 20 h culture in the fresh culture medium, the cellular activities were measured by CCK-8 assay as described above.

Followingly, CDT efficacy was assessed on 4T1 cells, with simultaneous treatment of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (0 or 100 μg/mL) and H2O2 at varying concentrations (0, 10, 25, 50, 75 and 100 μM) for 4 h. After another 20 h continuous cultivation in fresh medium, cellular activities were determined by standard CCK-8 assay.
Furthermore, the synergistic effect of PTT/CDT was determined in similar method, at the conditions that with both H2O2 (50 μM) and 660 nm laser illumination at intensity of 0.5 W/cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay and cell living/death evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells (2 × 105/mL) were seeded in the 48-well culture plate for attachment, various treatment of 100 μM H2O2 alone, 100 μg/mL PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs alone, and the mixture of them were performed, respectively. 4 h later, cells were cleaned triply by PBS and further cultured with fresh medium containing ROS fluorescence probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) (0.02 mM). The intracellular ROS signal was photographed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM800). Meanwhile, differently treated cells were beamed with 660 nm laser at intensity of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min, and co-stained with fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI) for cell living/death observation by the CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro tetramodal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells (1 × 105) were firstly seeded into a 35 mm confocal dish, and PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs or PEG-RLS/CDs (20 μg/mL) were then added for in vitro fluorescence imaging evaluation. After 24 h incubation, cells were cleaned by cold PBS twice to get rid of non-internalized assemblies, and observed by CLSM with 633 nm excitation.

In order to investigate the photothermal imaging, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs aqueous dispersions of different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, and 400 μg/mL) were firstly irradiated with a 660 nm laser at density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. In the meantime, real-time infrared thermal images and temperatures were recorded by the IR thermal camera (FLIR E60, UK).

The in vitro MRI tests were performed on a clinical MR scanner (3.0 T, Siemens, Germany) at 25 °C as previous reports [30]. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs were diluted to different concentrations (0, 0.007, 0.014, 0.028, 0.056, 0.28, 0.56 mM) in water for longitudinal relaxation rate (T1) test. Meanwhile, a T1 inversion recovery sequence was applied for the T1-weighted MR images, using the following parameters: repetition time (TR) values ranging from 20 to 1000 ms, echo time (TE) = 5.3 ms. The r1 value was computed through the curve fitting of the 1/T1 relaxation time (s−1) referenced by the Fe concentration (mM).

To investigate the in vitro photoacoustic (PA) imaging, a series of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs or PEG-RLS/CDs at indicated concentrations (0–500 μg/mL) were placed in the agarose tube. Afterward, the PA images and signals were recorded by a PA imaging platform (MSOT inVision 128, iTheraMedical, Germany) with an excitation wavelength ranging from 680 to 900 nm. The deionized water was applied as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tetramodal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Female Balb/c mice (4 weeks old, 15–20 g) were bought from Nanjing Gempharmatech Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). All animal experiments were approved and conducted in accordance with accepted standard of the Biomedical Research Ethics Committee of West China Hospital of Sichuan University. The 4T1 tumor model was established by subcutaneous injection of 4T1 cells (1 × 106 per mouse) into the back of mouse. When the volume of tumor reached to ~100 mm3, mice were applied for the following imaging analysis after anesthesia by intraperitoneal injection of chloral hydrate (10%, w/v, 100 µL/mice), or using isoflurane in oxygen flow using a Matrx VMS small animal anesthesia machine (Midmark, USA).

The whole-body fluorescent imaging was determined by an imaging system (CRI Maestro 2, USA) at 2, 4, 6, 24, 28 and 32 h post-injection with 630 nm excitation wavelength. After that, the tumor tissues and major organs were harvested and subjected to ex vivo fluorescence imaging for tissue distribution measurement.

Photoacoustic imaging was conducted on the same MSOT scanner as that for in vitro study. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (100 µL, 25 mg/kg) were intravenously injected with laser irradiation (excitation wavelengths of 680 to 900 nm) at indicated time. The PA signals of tumor sites were analyzed by the in-built software of inVision128.

Meanwhile, the photothermal imaging from PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was carried out after intravenously injected with PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (100 μL, 25 mg/kg). 28 h after injection, tumor sites were irradiated with a NIR laser (660 nm, 0.5 W/cm2, 5 min) with real-time imaging and record at indicated time points (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 min).

The in vivo MR imaging was acquired by the 3.0 T clinical MRI scanner (Siemens, Germany). Briefly, the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were scanned at 0, 6, 28, and 32 h post i.v. injection of the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (25 mg/kg). The MR images were acquired using the following scanning sequence: TR = 530 ms, TE = 30 ms, field-of-view (FOV) = 35 × 35 mm2, slice = 13, and thickness = 1 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal-enhanced CDT and transfection study in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: When the tumor grew to volume of ~100 mm3, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5 per group) with varied treatments: (1) PBS, (2) PBS plus laser, (3) PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs and (4) PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs plus laser. In the group 3 and 4, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was intravenously injected into the mice at a dosage of 25 mg/kg. After 28 h, tumors in groups 2 and 4 were exposed to 660 nm NIR laser (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min. The weight and tumor volume of each mouse (calculated as length × width2/2) was monitored every other day. Following 16-day treatments, the mice were sacrificed, and the histopathological analysis was evaluated by the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Furthermore, the survival time experiment of 4T1-tumor-bearing mice in four groups were performed (n = 5 per group) with different treatments within 50 d.

For transfection assay in vivo, when the tumor reached ~100 mm3, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 4), which received i.t. RLS/GFP, i.v. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/GFP and i.v. PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/GFP + NIR treatment. In each group, mice were injected with a dosage of 12.5 mg/kg EGFP plasmid DNA, respectively. 48 h after injection, the tumor tissues were immediately frozen in the O.T.C embedding agent with liquid nitrogen followed by cutting into 10 μm thick cryosections. The green fluorescence of cryosections were observed and captured by fluorescence microscopy and the MFI of tissue sections was analyzed by Image J software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and characterization of the Fe@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe@CDs was prepared by a solvothermal method using FePc as precursor [22], and CDs without Fe was set as control group, using Pc as precursor. The obtained Fe@CDs showed uniform dispersion with average diameter of ~ 2.3 nm by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation (Fig. 1 A). Further high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image illustrated that it also had high crystallinity. The lattice spacing (0.23 nm and 0.25 nm) were closer to the (100) phase of graphite carbon and the (311) diffraction facets of Fe3O4, respectively (Fig. 1B) [31,32]. The chemical composition investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed four typical peaks at 285, 400, 531, and 711 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe 2p, respectively (Fig. S1 A) [31,33]. The high-resolution spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. 1C) showed characteristic peaks at 711 and 724 eV from the Fe-O in Fe@CDs [33], while those of C1s, N1s, O1s were also studied (Fig. S1B, C and D). Besides, the presence of Fe3O4 was also confirmed in the high-resolution XPS spectrum by absence of Fe 2p3/2 satellite peak, which has been reported in previous literatures [34]. The functional groups on the surface of Fe@CDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. 1D). A broad peak at 3405 cm−1 suggested the presence of N-H in the Fe@CDs, and peaks at 1645 and 1564 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O/C = N and C = C, respectively [35]. Meanwhile, an apparent increasement of the stretching vibration of C-H at 3045 cm−1 was observed and compared with that of FePc, which might due to the larger conjugated degree in Fe@CDs [35]. And the obvious reduction of the stretching vibration of C-N-C at 1334 cm−1 from FePc to Fe@CDs, indicated the break of the bonds between C-N-C of FePc under high temperature and pressure [36]. Notably, Fe@CDs showed the characteristic Fe-O peak at 599 cm−1 [37].

Next, we studied the optical properties of Fe@CDs, including fluorescence spectrum and absorption spectrum. An obvious emission peak occurred in the deep-red region (λmax = 690 nm) under excitation at 650 nm (Fig. 1E) with wavelength-independent properties (Fig. S1E) due to the homogeneous microstructure and surface chemical states of Fe@CDs. [38,39] Fe@CDs showed peaks at 428–660 nm in the absorption spectra, which are ascribed to the n–π* transition of C = O, C = N and C-N-C bonds respectively. (Fig. 1F). Compared with FePc at 655 nm, the as-prepared Fe@CDs red-shifted to 660 nm probably because of the J-aggregates of aromatic structures in the Fe@CDs. As control, all the characterization and optical properties of CDs synthesized with Pc were also studied (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3A-C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Both DSPE-mPEG2000 and RLS were cooperated with carbon dots during self-assembly for water solubility improvement and gene delivery. It was noticeable that fluorescence emission of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was almost completely quenched (Fig. 1E) due to the fluorophore aggregation-caused quenching phenomenon, but it may favour high photothermal conversion efficiency [40,41]. The fluorescence can be recovered when the assemblies collapse in DMF (Fig. S1F). Compared with the absorption spectrum of Fe@CDs in DMF, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs exhibited red-shift from 660 to 700 nm and a broader absorption band in aqueous solution (Fig. 1F), probably ascribed to the more J-aggregates of aromatic structures in Fe@CDs [15,42]. Additionally, the morphology of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was showed a uniform spherical structure with diameter of ~ 77 nm (Fig. 1G) by TEM, and possessed a hydrodynamic diameter of 307 nm and ζ potential of + 25.8 mV (Fig. 1H). As illustrated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S4A), the characteristic peaks at 2921 and 1101 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of C-C and C-O of DSPE-mPEG2000 respectively, suggesting the successful surface functionalization with DSPE-mPEG2000. In contrast, the PEG-RLS/CDs presented similar characteristics in its fluorescence emission, UV–vis absorption, FTIR, morphology, size distribution and ζ potential (Fig. S3D-F, Fig. S4B).

From the agarose gel retardation assay, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs nanohybrids could condense DNA and completely retard gene migration as the N/P ratio increased to 5 (Fig. S5A). Meanwhile, the desired rapid gene release was evaluated in reductive environments [7]. After incubation with DTT solution (10 mM) for 2 h, clear released DNA bands were visible at all tested N/P ratios in the nanohybrids, indicating separation of gene and cationic carriers (Fig. S5A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties and POD-like catalytic performance of prepared carbon dots assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: After irradiation with 660 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for 10 min, the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs aqueous dispersion exhibited satisfying concentration-dependent photothermal effect (Fig. 2 A). The temperature could quickly rise from 24.3 to 52.3 °C at concentration of 400 μg/mL, compared with only 1.4 °C increase in the aqueous solution without nanohybrids. Meanwhile, CDs (in DMF) and PEG-RLS/CDs (in H2O) displayed no photothermal capacity (Fig. S6A and B), while the unencapsulated Fe@CDs (in DMF) showed excellent photothermal conversion property (Fig. S6C). Followingly, the photothermal stability evaluation by the irradiation-cooling cycles showed no obvious deterioration of the maximal raised temperature during process, revealing remarkable photostability. (Fig. 2B) More excitingly, after calculation, the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs reached up to 63.4% photothermal conversion efficiency (η). (Fig. S6D) It was higher than that of previously reported photothermal nanomaterials, such as 40.8% for hydrogenated black TiO2, 38.5% for polythiophene phenylpropionic acid-based CDs, 33.7% for bi/phthalocyanine manganese nanocomposite, 37% for Gd/Fe-bimetal-phenolic nanoparticle, 38.4% for Au nanorods and 27.4% for black phosphorus [27,43–47]. These compares confirmed that the difference must come from the Fe doping, solidifying the fact that the metal iron doped phthalocyanine based photothermal agents have excellent photothermal conversion such as photothermal-conversion efficiency of 58.3% and 72.3% in cruciform phthalocyanine and nanosheets of phthalocyanine, respectively [10,48].

In order to further explore the photothermal conversion mechanisms of Fe doping, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed on FePc (precursor of Fe@CDs) and Pc (precursor of CDs) to identify the intrinsic structural and electronic features [49]. It showed the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) distributions of the FePc and Pc by computational simulation (Fig. 2C). Both HOMO and LUMO orbitals of Pc were mainly contributed from the phthalocyanine rings. The LUMO orbitals of FePc were largely localized on the iron (II) ion, and those of the HOMO were still on phthalocyanine rings. The variated LUMO distribution led to the different band gap energy (Eg) of 2.14 eV for Pc and 1.60 eV for FePc. The lower band gap may result in the successful photothermal conversion of FePc based CDs under 660 nm irradiation (a photon of 1.879 eV), which subsequently induced recombines via nonradiative processes and produce heat [10]. While Pc with higher band gap energy could not be excited by the laser irradiation. This phenomenon was similar to the previous reports that under 808 nm irradiation (a photon of 1.535 eV) porphyrin-like metal organic framework with lower Eg of 1.31 eV can be excited while the reference Fe2O3 (Eg = 2.26 eV) cannot [49].

The POD-like catalytical property of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as nanozyme was determined by the reaction with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 2D) [29,50]. If PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs could catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radical (•OH), it can oxidate TMB to be blue with maximum absorbance at 652 nm [43]. As expected, neither TMB alone nor TMB with H2O2 (TMB + H2O2) showed detectable absorbance, indicating no oxidation reaction occurred (Fig. 2D). While an obvious characteristic absorbance peak at 652 nm could be observed in the presence of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, implying its intrinsic POD-like activity [51]. Moreover, the POD-like activity has been proved to be pH-dependent (Fig. S6E), with optimal nanozyme function at pH 2.0–5.5, which may favour the •OH generation in the tumor microenvironment [50]. On the other hand, no apparent absorbance peak was observed in the PEG-RLS/CDs group (Fig. S6F). In addition, methylene blue (MB) dye was applied to test the generation of •OH at different conditions (Fig. 2E). The chromogenic MB could transform to be colourless by the •OH oxidation, showing reduced absorption at 664 nm [51]. It was clear that, the absorbance was obviously decreased in the MB + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs + H2O2 group, compared with those in other conditions. And more interestingly, with temperature rise from 25 to 45 °C, degradation of MB increased from 36% to 80%, indicating a temperature-dependent •OH generation of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs. Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were also explored for •OH generation confirmation, using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. As displayed in Fig. 2F, a typical 1:2:2:1 multiple peak of •OH were obtained. Assessment at higher temperature (45 °C) exhibited a higher intensity of ESR, which was the same as the MB degradation experiment. These results are benefit to the further CDT/PTT synergistic therapy in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal-promotive gene transfection
ce:section.ce:para: Many previous literatures reported that the local heating can enhance endosomal escape during gene delivery process and promote the transfection efficiency [2,8]. Thus, we performed PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs/DNA complexes transfection in the absence or presence of laser irradiation to study the potential photothermal effect on gene delivery (Fig. 3 ). Initial optimization of gene transfection was performed at various weight ratios of DSPE-mPEG2000/RLS complex, revealing the highest transfection efficiency at the weight ratios of 0.5 (DSPE-mPEG2000 to RLS, P/R = 0.5) (Fig. S5B). Transfection with PEI 25 k, Lipo 2000 and RLS complexes with or without laser irradiation were used as contrast, and all of them showed negligible transfection fluctuation change (Fig. 3 and Fig. S5B). On the contrary, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs exhibited photothermally enhanced transfection efficiency with laser irradiation for 10 min (Fig. 3C). The transfection efficiency (N/P = 10, 20) was increased approximately 2~3.5 fold compared to non-irradiation group (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro FL/MR/PA/PT tetramodal imaging performance of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the intrinsic excellent fluorescent property of carbon dots, the cellular fluorescent imaging capability of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs and PEG-RLS/CDs are firstly investigated (Fig. 4 A). Both assemblies exhibited strong red signal in cytoplasm under the 633 nm excitation in 4T1 cells, indicating the fluorescence recovery of carbon dots due to the polymer disassembly in cell. Inspired by the T1-weighted MR imaging and low biotoxicity of carbon dots doped with transition metal ions (eg, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+ and Ni2+) [52], the relaxivity test and T1-weighted MR imaging of the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs were performed on a 3 T MRI scanner (Fig. 4B and C). After calculation, the longitudinal relaxivity (r1) of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs was 1.25 mM-1s−1 (Fig. 4B), and the T1-weighted MR imaging of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs exhibited obviously positive contrast signal getting brighter with the increased concentration (Fig. 4C). According to reported studies, the photothermal effect can generate acoustic waves that can be detected and converted into imaging signals, which is called photoacoustic imaging [53]. Thus, we next measured the PA imaging capacity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in an agarose gel phantom in the MSOT inVision 128 PA tomography system (Fig. 4D), showing a dose dependent signal enhancement. By contrast, no detectable signal could be detected in the PEG-RLS/CDs (Fig. S7B and Fig. 4E), probably attributed to the low near-infrared absorbance [54]. Followingly, the IR thermal camera was employed for PT imaging evaluation of prepared carbon dots assemblies. Comparing to H2O, the PT signal strength of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs significantly enhanced with dosage increase, raising up to 52.3 °C at 400 μg/mL for 10 min irradiation (Fig. 4F and G). While the PEG-RLS/CDs group nearly unchanged under the same condition (Fig. S7A and Fig. 4G). It confirmed better photothermal conversion efficiency of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs than PEG-RLS/CDs, consistent with previously detected photothermal properties (Fig. 2). On the whole, the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs performed distinctive PA/PT imaging modality relevant to PEG-RLS/CDs probably as changed electronic contribution and the band energy gap by the Fe doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and PTT/CDT synergistic therapy in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by high photothermal conversion property and considerable POD-like catalytic performance of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as nanozyme, the in vitro anti-cancer effect was further assessed. Satisfyingly, PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs performed stronger cytotoxic effect in 4T1 breast tumor cells than normal L929 fibroblast cell at high concentration (≥100 μg/mL) (Fig. 5 A, a&b and Fig. S8A).

It was probably because that much more •OH was induced through POD-like reaction inside the tumor cells with a relative high level H2O2 [55–57]. Further, 4T1 cells were irradiated at 660 nm for 10 min after 4 h PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs treatment, exhibiting anticancer effect even at relatively low dosage (25 μg/mL) with 16.4% cell viabilities decrease (Fig. 5A and Fig. S8B). At relatively high concentration (100 μg/mL), it showed satisfactory tumor cell suppression of 51%, which may result from both POD-like reaction and PTT effect [50]. For further simulating a higher H2O2 level inside the solid tumor tissues, H2O2 with gradient concentration was added (Fig. 5B). The PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs displayed enhanced CDT effect in the tumor cell mimicking H2O2 concentrations (50–100 μM), with 53.8% tumor inhibition at 100 μg/mL. The enhanced synergistic therapy effect was evaluated followingly with 100 μM H2O2. As expected, the combining group (H2O2 + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs + Laser) showed much higher tumor suppression effect (with ~ 80% cancer cells killing ratio) at the dose of 100 µg/mL, compared with the CDT alone group (H2O2 + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, with ~ 50% inhibition ratio) at the dose of 100 µg/mL (Fig. 5A, d&e and Fig. S8C). Thus, the •OH induction was further confirmed using DCFH-DA cellular probe which would emit green fluorescence by oxidation reaction (Fig. 5C). It was clear that strong green fluorescence was found only in the group of H2O2 + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, resulting from the successful •OH generation.

In the living/dead assay, the combination of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs with laser irradiation led to a large number of cells apoptosis. Moreover, the PTT/CDT synergistic therapy (H2O2 + PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs + Laser) could cause almost all cells propidium iodide (PI) positive staining, implying a more efficient apoptosis induction (Fig. 5D and Fig. S9). These results proved PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as a promising PTT/CDT synergistic agent for tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo FL/PA/PT/MR tetramodal imaging and PTT/CDT synergistic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous in vitro results, the multi-modal imaging and cancer therapy capacity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs were then investigated through the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. The fluorescence signals of tumor region gradually increased over time from 2 h to 28 h post i.v. administration of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs (25 mg/kg) (Fig. 6 A and B). After 28 h, the signals started to go down, which may be ascribed to the heterogenous materials metabolism. It also represented that 28-h post-injection was the most suitable time point for PA/PT/MR imaging detection and PTT/CDT synergistic therapy in vivo. To assess the distribution of the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs in vivo, tumor as well as major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were harvested and imaged after 32 h post-injection (Fig. S10A). The assembly mainly accumulated in the liver and lung tissues due to the macrophage uptake in the reticuloendothelial system [17]. Followingly, we studied the PA imaging performance of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs at various time points after i.v. injection. It was observed that PA signals were clearly improved at 2 h post-injection of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, which gradually increased till reaching the peak at 28 h post-injection. The PA signal intensities began to decrease at 28 h post-injection (Fig. 6C and D). Considering the excellent photothermal conversion efficiency in vitro, PT imaging in vivo was evaluated. After 28 h post-injection, the tumor tissue on mice were irradiated under a 660 nm laser at intensity of 0.5 W/cm2 for 5 min. It was noticeable that the temperature at tumor site rapidly increased from 35.6 to 51.4 °C in the PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs treated group, which is high enough to ablate tumors (Fig. 6E and F). While the temperature of tumor sites in the PBS + Laser group only increased to 39.4 °C within 5 min under the same irradiation conditions. Moreover, the capacity of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as T1-weighted contrast agent was also tested. The T1 MR signals at tumor site gradually increased and reached a plateau after 28 h post-injection (Fig. 6G and H), which was 2.4 times as high as that before injection. These results further proved the potentiality of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs as an excellent FL/PA/PT/MR tetramodal imaging agent.

Followingly, in vivo PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs based CDT/PTT synergistic tumor therapy was performed by utilizing a 660 nm irradiator (0.5 w/cm2) and intrinsic high H2O2 concentration in solid tumors (50–100 μM). The tumor volume was assessed throughout the whole study and the tumor tissues were stripped and imaged after the last treatment. Treatment of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs plus laser exhibited the most efficient tumor suppression effect (Fig. 7 A and B). On the contrary, the tumors of mice in the PBS and PBS plus laser groups grew rapidly and slightly tumor growth inhibition was observed in PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs alone treated group. The survival study also showing significant advantage of nanoprobe plus laser group, with 80% survival for at least 50 days (Fig. 7C). In the H&E staining, the most abundant necrotic tumor cells were detected in group of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs plus laser compared with other groups (Fig. 7D). Meanwhile, the body weight of all the mice were not significantly changed (Fig. S10B) and all the organs from the four groups showed no identifiable histopathological damage (Fig. S11), indicating a good biocompatibility of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal-responsive gene transfection
ce:section.ce:para: We further verified the in vivo gene delivery efficiency of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs based on photothermal therapy, which was performed in BALB/c mice bearing 4T1 tumor xenografts. As expected, RLS achieved the obvious fluorescence intensity in frozen tumor sections (Fig. 7E and Fig. S10C), which was aligned with our previous study [23]. Interestingly, evident green fluorescent protein expression was observed for PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs with laser irradiation, increased approximately 2-fold compared to the group without laser irradiation, consistent with the results of gene transfection in vitro. The results revealed that PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs have great potential as a safe, effective, and photothermal responsive delivery system for gene therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: For improvement of non-viral vectors based-tumor gene therapy, a novel liposomal carbon dots nanohybrids system (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) was herein synthesized, proving properties of enhanced gene transfection, in vivo visualization and synergistic cancer suppression. The included carbon dots (Fe@CDs) was firstly fabricated from FePc through Fe-doped strategy and solvothermal approach. After modification by the amphiphilic polymer (DSPE-mPEG2000) and RLS (a dendritic arginine and disulfide bond-containing cationic lipopeptide), the obtained Fe@CDs nanohybrids (PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs) exhibited good water solubility and biocompatibility. Supported by high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 63.4%) of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs, the nanohybrids obviously promoted the gene transfection efficiency both in vitro (in 4T1 cell, 3.5-fold increase) and in vivo (in 4T1 tumor tissue, 2-fold increase). The synthesized PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs could apply as a tetramodal contrast agent for FL/MR/PA/PT imaging, which could provide a diversified imaging-guided gene delivery diagnosis. The nanohybrids especially performed substantial signal enhancement on PA and PT imaging, with a three order of magnitude value and 16 °C temperature increase respectively. Moreover, the excellent photothermal ability of nanohybrids further facilitated a remarkable promotion of POD-like activity, resulting in an efficient PTT/CDT synergistic tumor inhibition both in vitro and in vivo. After treatment of PEG-RLS/Fe@CDs plus laser, tumor growth inhibition ratio increased by 83% with much longer survival (80% survival for at least 50 days). The designed nanohybrids provides a new way for gene delivery together with multimodal & real-time imaging in vivo, as well as PTT/CDT synergistic cancer therapy.
